According to Section 11 of the Labour Act 2004, if a contract has continued for more than two years but less than five years, the notice period is two weeks.

[Labour Act 2004, s.11(3)]

Since you have worked for 3 years, your employer is required to give you a notice period of two weeks.

[Labour Act 2004, s.11(3)]

rows.answer:
According to Section 11(2) of the Labour Act 2004, for a contract of employment that has continued for more than three months but less than two years, the notice period is one week. Since you have worked for 4 years, your contract falls under this category.

You are entitled to one week's notice from your employer. This means that your employer must give you a written notice of one week's duration before terminating your employment contract.

You can request your employer to provide you with the required notice period, and if they fail to do so, you may be entitled to claim damages or other remedies under the Labour Act.

[Labour Act 2004, s.11(2)]

rows.answer:
According to [Labour Act 2004, s.1(1)(a)], wages must be paid in legal tender and not otherwise. This means that employers are not allowed to pay wages in goods or store vouchers instead of money. 

If your employer attempts to pay you in goods or store vouchers, this would be in breach of the Labour Act 2004, s.1(1)(a).

rows.answer:
According to Section 13(3) of the Labour Act 2004, where a worker is at work for six hours or more a day, his work shall be interrupted (to the extent which is necessary having regard to its character and duration and to working conditions in general) by allowing one or more suitably spaced rest-intervals of not less than one hour on the aggregate.

However, the statutory text does not explicitly prohibit fines for lateness.

However, the statutory text does not explicitly prohibit fines for lateness.

rows.answer: According to [Labour Act 2004, s.5(7)], the total amount of deductions that may be made from the wages of a worker in any one month shall not exceed one-third of the wages of the worker for that month.

receipts.claim: According to [Labour Act 2004, s.5(7)], the total amount of deductions that may be made from the wages of a worker in any one month shall not exceed one-third of the wages of the worker for that month.

According to section 7(1)(a) of the Labour Act 2004, an employer must give a written statement to a worker within three months of the beginning of the worker's period of employment, detailing names, addresses, job nature, contract dates, notice periods, wages, and conditions. 

Since you started your new job two months ago, your employer is still within the three-month timeframe to provide you with a written statement. Therefore, your employer is obliged to give you a written statement as per section 7(1)(a) of the Labour Act 2004.

You are entitled to up to 12 days of paid sick leave annually for temporary illnesses, provided the employment contract is active and you submit to employer-nominated medical examination.

[Labour Act 2004, s.16]

receipts.claim: You are entitled to up to 12 days of paid sick leave annually for temporary illnesses, provided the employment contract is active and you submit to employer-nominated medical examination. [Labour Act 2004, s.16]

rows.answer:
According to [Labour Act 2004, s.16], a worker is entitled to be paid wages up to twelve working days in any one calendar year during absence from work caused by temporary illness certified by a registered medical practitioner.

Since you were sick for one week, you are entitled to be paid for that week. However, the Act states that you can only receive wages for the first day of the period in question, which in this case is the first day of your sickness.

receipts.claim: According to [Labour Act 2004, s.16], a worker is entitled to be paid wages up to twelve working days in any one calendar year during absence from work caused by temporary illness certified by a registered medical practitioner

rows.answer: According to Section 18 of the Labour Act 2004, a worker with 12 months of continuous service is entitled to a holiday with full pay of at least six working days.

receipts.claim: According to Section 18 of the Labour Act 2004, a worker with 12 months of continuous service is entitled to a holiday with full pay of at least six working days.

rows.answer:
According to [Labour Act 2004, s.18(3)], it is unlawful for an employer to pay wages in lieu of the holiday mentioned in subsection (1) of this section to a worker whose contract has not terminated.

This means that your boss cannot pay you extra money instead of letting you take your annual leave. You are entitled to take your annual leave as provided in the Labour Act, and your employer must respect this entitlement.

receipts.claim: According to [Labour Act 2004, s.18(3)], it is unlawful for an employer to pay wages in lieu of the holiday mentioned in subsection (1) of this section to a worker whose contract has not terminated

rows.answer:
The Labour Act 2004, s.7(1)(f) states that "the rates of wages and method of calculation thereof and the manner and periodicity of payment of wages" must be specified in the written employment particulars. However, it does not specify the exact frequency of payment.

The Employees' Compensation Act 2010, s.48, does not address the frequency of payment, but the Labour Act 2004, s.4(1) states that "no employer may make to a worker an advance of wages in excess of one month's wages." This implies that wages should be paid regularly, but the exact frequency is not specified.

The National Minimum Wage Act 2019, s.9(1) states that "an employer shall pay to the workers a wage not less than the national minimum wage, subject to statutory deductions." However, it does not specify the exact frequency of payment.

The Labour Act 2004, s.7(1)(f) also states that "the manner and periodicity of payment of wages" must be specified in the written employment particulars. However, it does not specify the exact frequency of payment.

The retrieved sections do not answer this question.

This implies that wages should be paid regularly, but the exact frequency is not specified

The Labour Act 2004, s.7(1)(f) also states that "the manner and periodicity of payment of wages" must be specified in the written employment particulars

rows.answer:
According to Section 4 of the Labour Act 2004, an employer is not allowed to make an advance of wages in excess of one month's wages. This means that if an employer has made an advance of wages that exceeds one month's wages, the worker is entitled to recover the excess amount within three months.

Section 4(1) states: "No employer may make to a worker an advance of wages in excess of one month's wages."

Section 4(2) states: "Where an advance in respect of wages has been paid to a worker the minimum period for the recovery of the advance by the employer shall be three months."

Therefore, the Labour Act 2004 protects you by allowing you to recover the excess advance amount within three months.

receipts.claim: According to Section 4 of the Labour Act 2004, an employer is not allowed to make an advance of wages in excess of one month's wages
This means that if an employer has made an advance of wages that exceeds one month's wages, the worker is entitled to recover the excess amount within three months
Section 4(1) states: "No employer may make to a worker an advance of wages in excess of one month's wages."
Section 4(2) states: "Where an advance in respect of wages has been paid to a worker the minimum period for the recovery of the advance by the employer shall be three months."

rows.answer:
According to Section 54 of the Labour Act 2004, a pregnant employee is entitled to:

1. Leave work six weeks before confinement if medically certified.
2. Not be permitted to work during the six weeks following her confinement.
3. Be paid not less than 50% of her wages if she is absent from work in pursuance of paragraph (a) or (b) of this subsection and had been continuously employed by her then employer for a period of six months or more immediately prior to her absence.
4. Be allowed half an hour twice a day during her working hours for nursing her child.

Leave work six weeks before confinement if medically certified

Since you have been continuously employed by your employer for a period of six months or more immediately prior to your absence, you are entitled to this benefit.

Additionally, Section 54(1)(d) states that you shall be allowed half an hour twice a day during your working hours for nursing your child.

rows.answer:
According to Section 10 of the Labour Act 2004, the transfer of employment contracts between employers requires the worker's consent and an authorized labor officer's endorsement. 

The officer must ensure that the worker's consent is freely given and may require a medical exam if the transfer changes the employment type or significantly alters conditions. 

Therefore, your employer cannot transfer you to a job in another city without your consent.

The officer must ensure that the worker's consent is freely given and may require a medical exam if the transfer changes the employment type or significantly alters conditions
Therefore, your employer cannot transfer you to a job in another city without your consent.

rows.answer:
According to Section 73 of the Labour Act 2004, forced labour is prohibited in Nigeria. Any person who requires or permits another person to perform forced labor contrary to the Nigerian Constitution is guilty of an offense and liable to a fine not exceeding N1,000 or to imprisonment for a period not exceeding two years, or to both.

Additionally, Section 73(2) specifically prohibits public officers from compelling people under their charge to work for private entities, with penalties ranging from a fine not exceeding N200 or to imprisonment for a period not exceeding six months, or to both.

Furthermore, Section 74 of the Labour Act 2004 empowers the Minister to regulate the requisition of labour for emergencies or communal obligations, but it does not explicitly prohibit forced labor in these situations. However, it does require consultation and agreement with the inhabitants of the town or village, and provides for exemptions and daily wages in lieu of labor.

receipts.claim: According to Section 73 of the Labour Act 2004, forced labour is prohibited in Nigeria

Any person who requires or permits another person to perform forced labor contrary to the Nigerian Constitution is guilty of an offense and liable to a fine not exceeding N1,000 or to imprisonment for a period not exceeding two years, or to both

Furthermore, Section 74 of the Labour Act 2004 empowers the Minister to regulate the requisition of labour for emergencies or communal obligations, but it does not explicitly prohibit forced labor in these situations
However, it does require consultation and agreement with the inhabitants of the town or village, and provides for exemptions and daily wages in lieu of labor.

rows.answer:
According to Section 13 of the NMW Act 2019, you can take your case directly to the National Industrial Court, or file a formal complaint with the Minister of Labour and Employment.

You can proceed as follows:

1. File a complaint with the Minister of Labour and Employment.
2. Alternatively, you can take your case directly to the National Industrial Court.

In either case, the Minister of Labour and Employment or the National Industrial Court will serve an enforcement notice on your employer, requiring them to pay the arrears of the wage to you within 30 days.

[NMW Act 2019, s.13]

rows.answer:
According to the Labour Act 2004, s.20, your employer must:

1. Inform the trade union or workers' representative concerned of the reasons for and the extent of the anticipated redundancy.
2. Adopt the principle of "last in, first out" in the discharge of the particular category of workers affected, subject to all factors of relative merit, including skill, ability and reliability.
3. Use their best endeavours to negotiate redundancy payments to any discharged workers who are not protected by regulations made under subsection (2) of this section.

[Labour Act 2004, s.20(1)(a), (b), (c)]

receipts.claim: According to the Labour Act 2004, s.20, your employer must:

1. Inform the trade union or workers' representative concerned of the reasons for and the extent of the anticipated redundancy. 2. Adopt the principle of "last in, first out" in the discharge of the particular category of workers affected, subject to all factors of relative merit, including skill, ability and reliability. 3. Use their best endeavours to negotiate redundancy payments to any discharged workers who are not protected by regulations made under subsection (2) of this section. [Labour Act 2004, s.20(1)(a), (b), (c)]

rows.answer: According to Section 3 of the National Minimum Wage Act, 2019, the current national minimum wage in Nigeria is N70,000.00 per month.

rows.answer:
According to Section 3 of the National Minimum Wage Act 2019, your employer must pay you a national minimum wage of not less than N70,000.00 per month. Since your monthly earnings are N45,000.00, which is less than the minimum wage, your employer is breaking the minimum wage law.

[Act name, s.X]

rows.answer:
According to Section 3 of the National Minimum Wage Act 2019, the national minimum wage of N70,000.00 per month is applicable to all workers under an employer's establishment, except as otherwise provided under this Act.

The Act does not specify who is excluded from the minimum wage, but it does provide exceptions elsewhere in the Act. However, Section 3(1) states that the national minimum wage is applicable to "every worker under his establishment".

Therefore, the 70,000 naira minimum wage covers all workers under an employer's establishment, but the Act provides for exceptions that are not specified in Section 3.

receipts.claim: According to Section 3 of the National Minimum Wage Act 2019, the national minimum wage of N70,000.00 per month is applicable to all workers under an employer's establishment, except as otherwise provided under this Act

rows.answer:
According to [NMW Act 2019, s.9(2)] and [NMW Act 2019, s.9(1)], if an employer fails to pay the national minimum wage, they are liable on conviction to pay:

(a) a fine not exceeding 5% of the offender's monthly wage;
(b) all outstanding arrears of the workers' wages; and
(c) an additional penalty of not less than the prevailing Central Bank of Nigeria lending rate on the wages owed, for each month of continuing violation, provided that the power to order payment on account of wages under subsection (2) shall not derogate from the right of the worker to recover wages due to him by any other proceeding in a court of competent jurisdiction.

receipts.claim: According to [NMW Act 2019, s.9(2)] and [NMW Act 2019, s.9(1)], if an employer fails to pay the national minimum wage, they are liable on conviction to pay:

According to [Employees' Compensation Act 2010, s.8], compensation is available for mental stress arising from workplace harassment, discrimination, or bullying, provided it leads to a mental or physical injury. However, it is not applicable if the employee's own actions or omissions contributed to the stress.

This means that you may be eligible for compensation for mental stress caused by your job, but you must have suffered a mental or physical injury as a result of the stress.

Therefore, your illness caused by chemical exposure at your factory job is likely covered under the Employees' Compensation Act 2010.

Therefore, you are still covered under the Act, as the injury occurred while performing a work-related activity or commuting.

However, [Employees' Compensation Act 2010, s.46] states that failure to comply with the Act's provisions, including paying assessed contributions or providing security, may result in penalties.
